column1: A Crash Course in Facial Fractures
column3: “The aspect I found most interesting in this course was the comprehensive integration of facial anatomy, fracture classification, and evidence-based management principles. The systematic approach to radiographic interpretation, combined with case-based discussions, offered a clear understanding of how clinical findings correlate with imaging. Additionally, the emphasis on interdisciplinary coordination in trauma care highlighted the complexity and precision required in managing facial fractures, making the overall learning experience both intellectually engaging and clinically relevant.”

Le format interactif et structuré, avec des points-clés, un quiz final et la possibilité de télécharger le support PDF. column1: Anaesthesia for Posterior Cranial Fossa Surgery
column3:
Most Interesting Aspect: Anaesthesia Technique
One of the most engaging aspects of the Anaesthesia for Posterior Cranial Fossa Surgery course was the detailed focus on anaesthesia technique specific to this challenging surgical area. The content effectively highlighted the unique physiological and anatomical considerations, particularly: Patient positioning (e.g., prone, sitting, park bench) and its implications for hemodynamics, venous return, and the risk of venous air embolism. The use of Total Intravenous Anesthesia (TIVA) with agents like propofol and remifentanil, which allow for better control of intracranial pressure and neurophysiological monitoring. Strategies for cerebral protection, such as controlled ventilation, normocapnia, and temperature management. The critical importance of preserving brainstem function, including anesthetic approaches that minimize interference with MEP and SSEP monitoring. These elements combined to provide a clear, evidence-based understanding of how anaesthesia in this region must be tailored to minimize complications and support optimal surgical outcomes.
column4: "One of the most engaging aspects of the Anaesthesia for Posterior Cranial Fossa Surgery course was the detailed focus on anaesthesia technique specific to this challenging surgical area. The content effectively highlighted the unique physiological and anatomical considerations. These elements combined to provide a clear, evidence-based understanding of how anaesthesia in this region must be tailored to minimize complications and support optimal surgical outcomes."

column3: I found the mechanisms of action of drugs to be the most interesting aspect of this course. Understanding how medications like antiepileptics block sodium or calcium channels, enhance GABA activity, or how NSAIDs selectively inhibit COX enzymes helped connect basic science to real clinical practice. I was also particularly interested in learning about drug indications beyond their primary use, such as Topiramate for migraine prevention and amphetamines for ADHD and narcolepsy. Seeing how pharmacologic principles translate into therapeutic decisions made the material practical and clinically relevant. Additionally, studying adverse effects and contraindications was valuable because it reinforces safe prescribing and patient-centered care
column4: "I found the mechanisms of action of drugs to be the most interesting aspect of this course. I was also particularly interested in learning about drug indications beyond their primary use, such as Topiramate for migraine prevention and amphetamines for ADHD and narcolepsy. Additionally, studying adverse effects and contraindications was valuable because it reinforces safe prescribing and patient-centered care."

column3:
One of the most interesting aspects of the course was learning about the triage system and how it helps prioritize patient care in high-pressure situations. Understanding how to quickly assess and categorize injuries using systems like START (Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment) gave me a clear picture of how critical thinking and rapid decision-making are applied in real-life emergencies.
I also found the team coordination and role assignment during mass casualty incidents particularly fascinating. It highlighted how effective communication and a structured chain of command can save lives during chaos. Additionally, the integration of mental health support for both victims and responders was a powerful reminder of the emotional impact of disasters.
column4: "One of the most interesting aspects of the course was learning about the triage system and how it helps prioritize patient care in high-pressure situations. Understanding how to quickly assess and categorize injuries using systems like START gave me a clear picture of how critical thinking and rapid decision-making are applied in real-life emergencies. column1: C-Arm Intraoperative Imaging for Ankle Fractures
column3: What I found most interesting in this course was learning how intraoperative imaging directly affects surgical outcomes especially in procedures like ankle fracture fixation. The use of the Mortise View to assess joint alignment and implant positioning was fascinating because I now understand how even a few millimeters of misalignment can lead to long-term complications. I also appreciated the practical knowledge on positioning the patient and the C-Arm, which are vital skills in the theatre setting. These insights will definitely help me support the surgical team more effectively during orthopedic cases.

These insights will definitely help me support the surgical team more effectively during orthopedic cases."

column3: I found several aspects of this course particularly interesting. First, the clear comparison between different types of chemical and radiological agents, their mechanisms, and characteristic symptoms. I also appreciated the detailed discussion of specific antidotes, such as atropine and pralidoxime for nerve agents or hydroxocobalamin and sodium thiosulfate for cyanide poisoning. Another highlight was learning that lifesaving interventions take priority over decontamination in irradiated or contaminated patients. The application of the START triage system to mass-casualty incidents was also very useful. I found the real-world clinical cases from Syria, Japan, and Goiânia especially engaging, as they connected theory with practice. Finally, the emphasis on hospital and community preparedness reinforced the importance of coordination and planning in disaster response.

column3: I found the sections on cognitive aids in crisis management most interesting. Learning how symptom- versus diagnosis-based aids can guide clinical decisions during high-stress situations really highlighted the importance of structured support in patient safety. I also appreciated the practical strategies for implementing and customizing cognitive aids in the OR, including adapting them to local protocols and ensuring team-wide buy-in. The discussions on malignant hyperthermia and local anesthetic toxicity management were particularly engaging, as they combined theoretical knowledge with step-by-step crisis interventions.

column3:
What I found most interesting about the Epidemiology – Fundamentals of Patient Safety (2024) course was the application of epidemiological principles to identify, analyze, and prevent patient safety incidents. The way concepts like incidence, prevalence, and risk factors were linked to real-world clinical errors made the subject highly practical and relevant.
I was particularly interested in understanding the distinction between errors of omission and commission, and how system-based factors contribute to these errors. The emphasis on a systems approach rather than individual blame was insightful and aligns well with modern healthcare quality improvement strategies.
Additionally, learning about tools such as root cause analysis, incident reporting systems, and risk mitigation strategies enhanced my understanding of how data can be used to improve patient outcomes and reduce harm.
Overall, the integration of epidemiology with patient safety provided a deeper perspective on delivering safer, evidence-based healthcare.
column4:

column3: I found this course on burn care extremely engaging because it covered the comprehensive management of patients, including physical, nutritional, and psychological aspects. I learned about fluid resuscitation using formulas like Parkland, and the importance of monitoring hydration in both adults and children. The course also deepened my understanding of burn assessment and classification, including depth, TBSA, and tools like the Rule of Palm and Rule of Nines. I was particularly interested in early surgical interventions, such as excision and skin grafting, and how they improve outcomes even in low-resource settings. Additionally, the focus on prevention, patient education, and family involvement, as well as the importance of teamwork, leadership, and communication, highlighted how multidisciplinary collaboration and quality improvement are essential for optimal burn care.
column4: "I found this course on burn care extremely engaging because it covered the comprehensive management of patients, including physical, nutritional, and psychological aspects."

column3:
I found the entire course to be engaging and informative. However, I think the most interesting aspects were the sections on:
1. Airway management: I found the different techniques and devices used to establish and maintain a patent airway fascinating.
2. Patient assessment: Learning about the various methods for assessing patients, including the AVPU scale and the Glasgow Coma Scale, was really interesting.
3. Vital signs: Understanding the importance of monitoring vital signs, such as pulse, breathing rate, and blood pressure, and how they can indicate changes in a patient's condition was really valuable.
4. Medical emergencies: The sections on responding to medical emergencies like cardiac arrest, stroke, and anaphylaxis were particularly interesting and informative.
Overall, the course provided a comprehensive overview of the skills and knowledge required to provide emergency care, and I found it all fascinating!
column4: "Overall, the course provided a comprehensive overview of the skills and knowledge required to provide emergency care, and I found it all fascinating!"

column3: The aspect of this course I found most interesting was the early recognition of critically ill patients and the emphasis on providing timely, life-saving interventions using low-cost and readily available resources. I was particularly impressed by how the course focused on simple, practical steps that can be applied in any healthcare setting, even those with limited equipment or personnel. It made me more confident in identifying danger signs early, prioritizing patients based on urgency, and responding quickly using essential emergency actions. I also appreciated the structured approach to “Recognize – Respond – Rescue,” which helps improve outcomes and reduce preventable deaths.
column4: "I was particularly impressed by how the course focused on simple, practical steps that can be applied in any healthcare setting, even those with limited equipment or personnel. It made me more confident in identifying danger signs early, prioritizing patients based on urgency, and responding quickly using essential emergency actions."

column3: I found the integration of anatomy, timing, and surgical decision-making in facial reanimation to be the most interesting part of the course. Understanding how lesion location and duration of denervation directly influence the choice between nerve repair, nerve transfer, or free muscle transfer gave me a clear, algorithmic framework for approaching patients with facial paralysis. I was also particularly engaged by the case presentations, which illustrated real clinical decision-making and highlighted both the technical and functional outcomes of different reconstructive strategies.

column3: I found the course most interesting because it highlighted how simple practices, like proper hand hygiene and safe medication handling, can significantly reduce hospital-acquired infections. I also appreciated learning evidence-based guidelines from WHO and CDC, and seeing how they apply directly to real-life clinical settings. The practical scenarios and clear explanations made the material engaging and highly relevant to patient safety.

The practical scenarios and clear explanations made the material engaging and highly relevant to patient safety."

column3: I found the focus on infection prevention and patient safety most interesting—learning how practices like hand hygiene, PPE use, and safe medication handling can significantly reduce hospital-acquired infections. I also appreciated seeing how evidence-based guidelines and ethical considerations come together in everyday clinical care."

column3: I found the aspects related to infection prevention and control most interesting, especially the evidence-based practices like the WHO’s 5 moments of hand hygiene, safe injection practices, and central line/urinary catheter bundles. These topics stood out because they are highly practical, directly applicable in daily clinical work, and have a major impact on patient safety and reduction of hospital-acquired infections (HAIs).
column4: "I found the aspects related to infection prevention and control most interesting, especially the evidence-based practices like the WHO’s 5 moments of hand hygiene, safe injection practices, and central line/urinary catheter bundles. These topics stood out because they are highly practical, directly applicable in daily clinical work, and have a major impact on patient safety and reduction of hospital-acquired infections (HAIs)."

column3: What I found most interesting about this course was how small, routine practices—like proper hand hygiene and careful doffing of PPE—can significantly impact patient outcomes and prevent hospital-acquired infections. The real-world relevance of these measures, supported by CDC and WHO guidelines, made the course both practical and enlightening. I was particularly surprised by how often self-contamination occurs during PPE removal and how critical it is to follow each step correctly. Learning about medication preparation practices, safe catheter use, and central line precautions also deepened my appreciation for the role anesthesiologists and other healthcare providers play in infection prevention.
column4: "What I found most interesting about this course was how small, routine practices—like proper hand hygiene and careful doffing of PPE—can significantly impact patient outcomes and prevent hospital-acquired infections. The real-world relevance of these measures, supported by CDC and WHO guidelines, made the course both practical and enlightening. I was particularly surprised by how often self-contamination occurs during PPE removal and how critical it is to follow each step correctly. Learning about medication preparation practices, safe catheter use, and central line precautions also deepened my appreciation for the role anesthesiologists and other healthcare providers play in infection prevention."

column3: In the Infection Control and Prevention course, I found the understanding of the chain of infection particularly interesting, as it provided a clear framework for how infections spread and how each link can be interrupted to prevent transmission. I also found the discussion on evidence-based practices engaging, especially how they are implemented to reduce healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) and improve patient outcomes. The section on proper use of PPE, especially the protocols for donning and doffing, was both practical and essential for ensuring safety in healthcare settings. Finally, the focus on hand hygiene as one of the most effective, yet simple, preventive measures really resonated with me, reinforcing its importance in everyday clinical practice.

I also found the discussion on evidence-based practices engaging, especially how they are implemented to reduce healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) and improve patient outcomes. The section on proper use of PPE, especially the protocols for donning and doffing, was both practical and essential for ensuring safety in healthcare settings. Finally, the focus on hand hygiene as one of the most effective, yet simple, preventive measures really resonated with me, reinforcing its importance in everyday clinical practice."

column3: I was pleased to learn that there are many people globally who have dedicated their careers to cater to LMICs. Although I knew many people had the same common sense as me (that healthcare is severely underfunded and inaccessible to the majority of the world population), it is amazing to see that people from all different backgrounds have the same goal of successfully making surgery and other aspects of healthcare a basic human right, regardless of financial or personal background.
column4: "I was pleased to learn that there are many people globally who have dedicated their careers to cater to LMICs. Although I knew many people had the same common sense as me (that healthcare is severely underfunded and inaccessible to the majority of the world population), it is amazing to see that people from all different backgrounds have the same goal of successfully making surgery and other aspects of healthcare a basic human right, regardless of financial or personal background."

column3: This course felt less like a short training and more like a foundational diploma in global surgery. It answered many of the key questions someone new to the field might have, while also providing depth and direction for further learning. The quality of the curriculum design, the choice of panelists, moderation, and accompanying readings was exceptional. It gave me clarity, confidence, and a sense of belonging within the global surgery conversation. I expect to revisit the materials repeatedly and would highly recommend this course to anyone interested in global surgery or health systems strengthening.
column4: "This course felt less like a short training and more like a foundational diploma in global surgery. It answered many of the key questions someone new to the field might have, while also providing depth and direction for further learning. The quality of the curriculum design, the choice of panelists, moderation, and accompanying readings was exceptional. It gave me clarity, confidence, and a sense of belonging within the global surgery conversation. I expect to revisit the materials repeatedly and would highly recommend this course to anyone interested in global surgery or health systems strengthening."

column3: I really appreciated how the global surgery course combined evidence based knowledge with practical insights, highlighting key aspects like surgical systems strengthening, workforce development, and innovative solutions for low resource settings. The course offered a well rounded perspective on the challenges and opportunities in global surgery, making it both informative and engaging.
column4: "I really appreciated how the global surgery course combined evidence based knowledge with practical insights, highlighting key aspects like surgical systems strengthening, workforce development, and innovative solutions for low resource settings. The course offered a well rounded perspective on the challenges and opportunities in global surgery, making it both informative and engaging."

column3: I found the systems-level framing of global surgery especially interesting, particularly how surgery was discussed not just as a clinical service, but as a core component of health systems, financing, workforce development, and equity. The sessions on financing surgical care, training models like COSECSA/PAACS, and the roles of different stakeholders (governments, nonprofits, academia, industry) helped me see how policy, economics, and implementation intersect in real-world settings. I also appreciated the emphasis on locally led solutions and avoiding extractive or short-term approaches.
column4: "I found the systems-level framing of global surgery especially interesting, particularly how surgery was discussed not just as a clinical service, but as a core component of health systems, financing, workforce development, and equity. The sessions on financing surgical care, training models like COSECSA/PAACS, and the roles of different stakeholders (governments, nonprofits, academia, industry) helped me see how policy, economics, and implementation intersect in real-world settings. I also appreciated the emphasis on locally led solutions and avoiding extractive or short-term approaches."

column3: I found the course particularly interesting for how it clearly mapped the global surgery ecosystem and explained the roles of different stakeholders, including NGOs, academic institutions, governments, and international organizations. The sections that linked global surgery principles to real-world implementation and advocacy were especially valuable, as they helped me understand how theoretical concepts translate into practical action. I also appreciated how the course emphasized collaboration, equity, and sustainability in global surgery initiatives

column3:
I found the most interesting aspect of this course to be the way it connected the technical components of surgical care with the systems-level thinking required in global health. The discussions on how surgery functions as an essential health system intervention, rather than a specialty confined to tertiary hospitals, were particularly powerful.
I was also very engaged by the sections on stakeholder mapping, especially understanding how clinicians, governments, nonprofits, academia, and industry all contribute distinct but interdependent roles in strengthening surgical systems. This helped me appreciate why global surgery requires coordinated, multisectoral action rather than isolated efforts.
Another compelling aspect was the emphasis on equitable research collaboration and avoiding surgical colonialism. The conversations about horizontal leadership, shared ownership, and contextualised research resonated deeply with me, especially as someone committed to ethical global health practice.
Finally, the modules on digital innovation, frugal technology, and the future role of AI in expanding surgical education and access were exciting. They showed how LMICs can leapfrog traditional barriers and adopt scalable, context-appropriate solutions, which aligns closely with my interests in health systems, innovation, and sustainable capacity-building.
Overall, the course offered a comprehensive, forward-thinking view of global surgery that blended advocacy, policy, technology, research, and training—making it both intellectually stimulating and practically empowering.

column1: Obstetrics in the Setting of Remote Retrieval Medicine
column3:
The modules on postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) were especially powerful—not just for the protocols, but for the way they emphasized early recognition, teamwork, and the human impact of every decision. Learning about the role of tranexamic acid, the limitations of visual blood loss estimation, and the importance of reflex monitoring during magnesium therapy gave me tools I can use immediately in practice.
But beyond the facts, what stood out was the emotional intelligence woven into the training—reminding us that normal vital signs can be misleading, and that vigilance saves lives. It made me feel more prepared, more empowered, and more connected to the mission of safe maternal care.

"The modules on postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) were especially powerful—not just for the protocols, but for the way they emphasized early recognition, teamwork, and the human impact of every decision. This course enhanced my ability to approach skin lesions systematically and reinforced the importance of timely diagnosis in improving patient outcomes.

column3: 1. The Role of Laparoscopy in Trauma Laparoscopy’s high specificity (100%) makes it a powerful tool for diagnosing diaphragmatic injuries that might be missed on imaging. The ability to directly visualize injuries and avoid unnecessary laparotomies is a game-changer in trauma surgery. 2. Diaphragmatic Injuries & the Thoracoabdominal Region The thoracoabdominal region (4th ICS to inferior costal margin) is tricky because injuries can involve both the chest and abdomen. Delayed diaphragmatic herniation is a real concern, making early diagnosis crucial. 3. Hemoperitoneum Grading & Its Importance Learning to grade hemoperitoneum (from small flecks of blood to large clot formation) helps in decision-making for surgical intervention. Grade 1 (small flecks of blood) might not require major intervention, whereas Grade 3 (large clots) suggests significant bleeding needing urgent action. 4. Managing Penetrating Trauma & Surgical Decision-Making Knowing when to repair vs. observe vs. drain (e.g., diaphragm laceration) is a key surgical skill. Thoracostomy tube placement is essential in some cases to prevent pneumothorax when dealing with thoracic injuries.

Diaphragmatic Injuries & the Thoracoabdominal Region The thoracoabdominal region (4th ICS to inferior costal margin) is tricky because injuries can involve both the chest and abdomen. Delayed diaphragmatic herniation is a real concern, making early diagnosis crucial. 3. Hemoperitoneum Grading & Its Importance Learning to grade hemoperitoneum (from small flecks of blood to large clot formation) helps in decision-making for surgical intervention. Grade 1 (small flecks of blood) might not require major intervention, whereas Grade 3 (large clots) suggests significant bleeding needing urgent action. 4. Thoracostomy tube placement is essential in some cases to prevent pneumothorax when dealing with thoracic injuries."

column3: I found several aspects of this course particularly interesting and valuable. First, the detailed explanation of different types of sutures, including absorbable and non-absorbable materials, helped me understand their specific indications and appropriate uses. The step-by-step demonstration of various suturing techniques, such as interrupted, continuous, and intradermal stitches, was very helpful in visualizing the practical application. Additionally, learning about the selection of sutures based on tissue type and wound condition, especially the guidance on choosing sutures for infected versus clean wounds, enhanced my clinical reasoning. Finally, the emphasis on preventing infection and ensuring optimal wound healing through proper technique and suture choice was very insightful for my future medical practice.
column4: "I found several aspects of this course particularly interesting and valuable. The detailed explanation of different types of sutures helped me understand their specific indications and appropriate uses. The step-by-step demonstration of various suturing techniques, was very helpful in visualizing the practical application. Learning about the selection of sutures based on tissue type and wound condition enhanced my clinical reasoning. Finally, the emphasis on preventing infection and ensuring optimal wound healing through proper technique and suture choice was very insightful for my future medical practice."

column3: One of the most interesting aspects of this course was learning about different types of sutures and their specific clinical applications. Understanding the differences between absorbable vs. non-absorbable, monofilament vs. multifilament, and knowing when to use each type in various surgical scenarios—such as infected wounds, vascular repair, or subcutaneous closure—was both practical and insightful. These concepts are directly applicable in clinical settings and enhance surgical decision-making skills.
column4: "One of the most interesting aspects of this course was learning about different types of sutures and their specific clinical applications. Understanding the differences between absorbable vs. non-absorbable, monofilament vs. multifilament, and knowing when to use each type in various surgical scenarios—such as infected wounds, vascular repair, or subcutaneous closure—was both practical and insightful. column3: I was fascinated when I read the below statement; Arterial blood pressure measurement is more accurate than measurement of a blood pressure BP by a non-invasive cuff, especially in hypotensive patients or patients with heart arrhythmias. With an arterial BP measurement the systolic pressure will read slightly higher and diastolic pressure slightly lower (5–10 mmHg), when compared to the same non-invasive measurements. It gives beat by beat near real-time measurement which permits the rapid recognition of blood pressure changes. Arterial cannulation allows repeated arterial blood gas samples to be drawn. It can be used where non-invasive cuffs are difficult. Two examples are burns and bariatrics cases.
column4: "I was fascinated when I read the below statement; Arterial blood pressure measurement is more accurate than measurement of a blood pressure BP by a non-invasive cuff, especially in hypotensive patients or patients with heart arrhythmias. column3: Z-plasty is a fascinating technique, especially for improving the aesthetic outcomes of scars and addressing contracture. The ability to change the direction of a scar or lengthen it to enhance function is quite useful. Were there specific aspects of Z-plasty that stood out to you, like the flap design or the impact on scar formation?

column3: The most interesting part was how Z-plasty design directly changes scar behavior. Seeing how adjusting limb angles (like 30–60°) and lengths actually affects tension, lengthening, and final scar orientation made it feel very practical, not just theory.
